content: Hello! Today we're going to work on fractions. Are you ready to get started?
Hi! Yes I'm ready. I've been looking forward to this.
That's great to hear! Let's start with a question. Do you know what a fraction represents?
I think it's like a part of something? Like half a pizza?
Excellent example! That's exactly right. A fraction represents a part of a whole. The top number is the numerator and the bottom is the denominator.
Oh okay so in one half the 1 is the numerator and 2 is the denominator?
Perfect! You've got it. Now can you tell me what three quarters would look like as a fraction?
Would it be 3 over 4? Like 3/4?
Brilliant! You're picking this up really fast. Now here's a slightly harder one. What fraction of a pizza is left if you eat 2 slices out of 8?
That would be 6/8 right? Because 8 minus 2 is 6. Oh wait could that also be 3/4?
Wow that's an outstanding observation! You just discovered equivalent fractions all on your own. 6/8 and 3/4 are the same amount.
That's so cool! So you can simplify fractions by dividing both numbers by the same thing?
Welcome back! Today we're going to review decimals. What do you remember from last time?
Hi.
Can you tell me what a decimal is?
Not really.
That's okay. A decimal is another way to represent parts of a whole using a dot. For example 0.5 is the same as one half.
Okay.
Let me try an example. If you have 0.25 of a dollar how many cents is that?
I don't know. 25 maybe?
That's actually correct! 0.25 of a dollar is 25 cents. Can you think of why?
Because there are 100 cents in a dollar?
Yes exactly! You see you do understand more than you think. Each decimal place represents a fraction of 100.
I guess that makes sense.
Hi there! Let's work on multiplication today. How do you feel about times tables?
Hello! I've actually been practising my times tables at home. Can I show you?
Of course! Go ahead.
So 7 times 8 is 56 and 9 times 6 is 54 and 12 times 11 is 132!
Wow fantastic job! Those are all correct. I'm really impressed that you've been practising on your own. That shows great initiative.
Thanks! I made flashcards. Can we try some harder ones? What about multiplying two digit numbers?
I love your enthusiasm! Let's try 23 times 4. What strategy would you use?
I'd break it up. 20 times 4 is 80 and 3 times 4 is 12 so 80 plus 12 is 92.
That's a superb strategy! Breaking numbers apart like that is called the distributive property. You figured it out intuitively.
Can we try a bigger one? What about 35 times 12?
Sure! Give it a try using the same approach.
Okay so 35 times 10 is 350 and 35 times 2 is 70 so 350 plus 70 is 420?
Absolutely perfect! You're really showing mastery here. I'm so proud of the work you've put in.
Today we're going to learn about telling time on an analogue clock. Have you seen one before?
I think my grandma has one on her wall. The one with the hands?
That's right. An analogue clock has two hands. The short one shows the hour and the long one shows the minutes. Where does the short hand point at 3 o'clock?
At the 3?
Correct! Now here's the tricky part. Where does the long hand point at 3 o'clock?
Hmm at the 3 too?
Not quite. At any exact hour like 3 o'clock the long hand always points straight up at the 12. That means zero extra minutes past the hour.
Oh that's confusing. So the long hand at 12 means it's exactly on the hour?
Exactly! I know it seems confusing at first but you'll get used to it. Now what if the long hand points at the 6. How many minutes past the hour would that be?
Is it 6 minutes?
Close but remember each number on the clock represents 5 minutes. So at 6 it's actually 30 minutes which we call half past.
Oh! Because 6 times 5 is 30. So if it pointed at the 3 that would be 15 minutes?
That's wonderful reasoning! Yes exactly. And 15 minutes past is called quarter past. You're making great connections.
Let's talk about shapes today. Can you name any shapes you know?
Um. Square. Circle.
Good start! Those are both shapes we see every day. Can you think of any others?
Triangle I guess.
Right. Now what makes a triangle different from a square?
It has three sides?
That's correct. A triangle has three sides and a square has four. What about a pentagon? Do you know how many sides it has?
No.
A pentagon has five sides. The prefix penta means five. Can you think of anything else with that prefix?
The Pentagon building in America?
Good connection! That building is shaped like a pentagon. Now a hexagon has six sides. What do you think an octagon has?
Eight? Like an octopus has eight legs?
That's a very clever link! Yes exactly eight sides. You're good at finding these patterns.
Today we're going to work on word problems. I'll read one and you try to figure out the answer.
Okay but I find word problems really hard. I never know what to do.
That's alright. We'll take it step by step. Here's the first one. Sarah has 12 apples. She gives 3 to her friend. How many does she have left?
That's just 12 minus 3. It's 9.
See you can do it! The key word was gives which tells us to subtract. Let's try a harder one. Tom has 4 bags with 6 marbles in each bag. How many marbles does he have in total?
Oh so 4 times 6 is 24 marbles?
Fantastic! You identified it as a multiplication problem. What gave it away?
You said each bag has 6 and there are 4 bags. So it's like adding 6 four times which is the same as multiplying.
That's an excellent explanation! You really understand the connection between addition and multiplication. I'm very impressed with how you broke that down.
Can I try to write my own word problem?
I'd love that! Go ahead.
Okay. A library has 5 shelves and each shelf has 8 books. If someone borrows 3 books how many are left?
What a creative problem! That combines multiplication and subtraction. Can you solve your own problem?
5 times 8 is 40 books and then 40 minus 3 is 37 books left!
Perfect! I'm really proud of you for creating and solving your own multi-step problem. That's impressive work.
Let's review measurement today. Do you know the difference between centimetres and metres?
Metres are bigger than centimetres.
Right. Do you know how many centimetres make up one metre?
Is it 10?
Not quite. There are actually 100 centimetres in one metre. Think of it like cents in a dollar. The prefix centi means one hundredth.
Oh like century means 100 years?
Great thinking! You're making a really good connection there. Now if your desk is 120 centimetres long how many metres is that?
Hmm 120 divided by 100 is 1.2 so 1.2 metres?
Excellent work! You converted that perfectly. Now what about millimetres? There are 10 millimetres in a centimetre.
So there must be 1000 millimetres in a metre? Because 100 times 10 is 1000?
Outstanding! That's exactly right. And that's actually where the prefix milli comes from. It means one thousandth.
I'm starting to see how all the prefixes work together. Milli centi and then the base unit.
You've just described the metric system beautifully. That's a really mature understanding of how measurement works.
Today let's work on reading comprehension. I'm going to read you a short passage and ask some questions.
I don't like reading.
That's okay. Let's try something short. The cat sat on the warm mat by the fire. It purred softly as the rain fell outside. What is the cat doing?
Sitting on a mat.
Good. And how do you think the cat feels? What word gives us a clue?
Warm?
Warm does describe the mat but think about what the cat is doing. It purred softly. What does purring usually mean?
That it's happy?
That's right! When we figure out something the text doesn't say directly that's called making an inference. You just made one.
I didn't know that had a special name.
It does. Let me ask another inference question. Why do you think the cat chose to sit by the fire?
Because it was raining and cold outside so the fire is warm?
That's a thoughtful answer. You used clues from the passage to figure that out. That's exactly what good readers do.
Hi! Today we're going to learn about data and graphs. Have you ever seen a bar chart?
Yeah we did one in school about favourite colours! I really liked making it. Mine was the tallest because lots of people picked blue.

That sounds fun! So you already know that the height of each bar shows a count. What if I told you our class voted on favourite fruits. Apples got 8 votes bananas got 5 and grapes got 3. Which bar would be tallest?
Apples because 8 is the most. And grapes would be the shortest.
Perfect! Now here's a question that requires a bit more thinking. How many students voted in total?
8 plus 5 plus 3 is 16 students!
Well done! Now what if I asked what fraction of students chose bananas?
Oh that's using what we learned before! It would be 5 out of 16 so 5/16.
Brilliant! I love how you connected today's lesson to what we learned about fractions. That's exactly the kind of thinking that makes a strong mathematician.
What about percentages? Could we do that too? Like what percent chose apples?
Great question! You'd divide 8 by 16 which gives 0.5 and then multiply by 100 to get 50 percent. Can you try the percentage for bananas?
5 divided by 16 is 0.3125 times 100 is 31.25 percent?
That's exactly right! I'm really impressed that you calculated that so quickly and accurately.
This is fun! Can we make our own survey and graph the results next time?
Absolutely! That's a wonderful idea. I'll bring some graph paper next session.
Let's practice subtraction with borrowing today. Do you know how to solve 42 minus 17?
Um I know the answer is supposed to be 25 but I don't really understand the borrowing part.
That's honest and helpful to know. Let me walk through it. When we look at the ones column we have 2 minus 7. Can we do that?
No because 2 is smaller than 7.
Right so we need to borrow from the tens column. We take one ten from the 4 making it 3 and add 10 to the ones column making it 12. Now what's 12 minus 7?
12 minus 7 is 5!
And what's left in the tens column?
3 minus 1 is 2. So the answer is 25!
You've got it! Let me give you another one. Try 63 minus 28.
Okay so 3 minus 8 I can't do that. Borrow from the 6 to make it 5 and then 13 minus 8 is 5. Then 5 minus 2 is 3. So 35!
That's absolutely correct and you explained every step perfectly! You clearly understand the process now.
Can I try a three digit one? What about 305 minus 168?
Go for it! That one's a bit trickier because you might need to borrow more than once.
Okay 5 minus 8 can't do it. But the tens column is 0 so I can't borrow from there either. I need to go to the hundreds first?
Exactly! You recognised the double borrowing situation. That's a really important insight. Take it step by step.
So 3 becomes 2 and the 0 becomes 10. Then I borrow from that 10 to make it 9 and the 5 becomes 15. So 15 minus 8 is 7 and 9 minus 6 is 3 and 2 minus 1 is 1. The answer is 137!
That's perfect! I'm genuinely amazed at how you worked through that. You didn't just get the answer right you understood exactly why each step works.
Today we need to go over your test results from last week. You scored 4 out of 10 on the fractions section.
Yeah I know I didn't do well. I got confused during the test.
Let's look at what went wrong. On question 3 you wrote that 1/3 is bigger than 1/2. Can you explain your thinking?
I thought 3 is bigger than 2 so 1/3 is bigger.
I see where the confusion is. With fractions the bigger the denominator the smaller each piece. Think of a pizza. Would you rather have it cut into 2 pieces or 3 pieces if you only get one piece?
Oh! 2 pieces because each piece would be bigger.
That's right! So 1/2 is actually larger than 1/3. This is a very common mistake so don't feel bad. Let's practice a few more. Which is bigger 1/4 or 1/5?
1/4 because 4 is smaller than 5 so each piece is bigger?
Good you're applying the rule correctly now. What about comparing 2/5 and 3/5?
That one's easier. 3/5 because the denominators are the same so you just compare the tops. 3 is bigger than 2.
Excellent! Now you're getting it. The key is knowing when to compare numerators and when to compare denominators. What about 3/4 and 2/3?
That one's harder because both numbers are different. I'm not sure.
That's okay. One strategy is to find a common denominator. What number do both 4 and 3 go into?
12? Because 4 times 3 is 12.
So 3/4 becomes 9/12 and 2/3 becomes 8/12. Now which is bigger?
9/12! So 3/4 is bigger than 2/3. That actually makes sense now.
Good work! I can see real improvement already just in this session. Keep practising and the next test will go much better.
Let's learn about symmetry today. A shape has symmetry if you can fold it in half and both sides match. Can you think of a letter that's symmetrical?
The letter A! If you fold it down the middle both sides are the same.
That's a perfect example! What about another one?
M and T and O. Oh and B has it too but going sideways!
Wonderful! You've noticed something really important. B has a horizontal line of symmetry while A has a vertical one. Some shapes can have more than one line of symmetry.
Like O! You could fold it any way and it would match because it's a circle.
That's a brilliant observation! A circle has infinite lines of symmetry. How many lines of symmetry do you think a square has?
Two? One down the middle and one across?
Those are two but there are actually more. Think about folding the square from corner to corner.
Oh the diagonals! So that's four total for a square?
Exactly four! You're thinking about this really carefully. Now what about a rectangle that isn't a square?
Hmm two? Across and down but not diagonal because the sides aren't equal length?
That's exactly right and your reasoning is spot on. The diagonals don't work because a rectangle has different length sides. I'm very impressed with your spatial reasoning today.
